notes.text: The H1 title does not contain any of this week's primary SEO keywords (e.g. 'fintech AI', 'AI risk management finance', 'AI trading algorithms'). Retitle to something like 'Fintech AI Regulatory Gap: How Agentic Systems Are Outpacing Compliance Frameworks in 2026' to capture target keyword traffic without sacrificing editorial voice.

The Sources section lists only two URLs (Taylor Wessing and FinTech Futures), but the article body cites at least five named sources — FinRegLab, Debevoise & Plimpton, Hogan Lovells, Braithwaite, and Klarna company filings. Every inline citation must have a corresponding URL in the Sources section; missing links undermine credibility and prevent editors from verifying claims.

The stat-box citing Forbes contributor Zenon Kapron ('5% of intraday liquidity buffers... by year-end 2025') is a single-contributor Forbes piece, not a primary research source. For a statistic this specific and consequential, cite the underlying research report directly, or add a qualifying phrase such as 'per Forbes contributor Zenon Kapron, citing [underlying source]' to flag its secondary-source nature to readers.

This article covers AI Investment Strategy / Capital Markets, which the Marketing Director has explicitly flagged as off-limits this week ('we do not yet have enough domain authority to compete in those SERPs — focus depth over breadth'). Before writing, always check the 'Content Pillar Focus This Week' section of the SEO guidelines. This week's assignment is AI in Finance Operations: fraud detection, credit scoring, and compliance automation. An otherwise well-written article was rejected solely because it targeted the wrong pillar at the wrong time.

The H1 ('Morgan Stanley's 2026 AI Breakthrough: What It Means for Tech Valuations') contains neither the required primary keyword nor follows the SEO H1 formula ([Primary Keyword]: [Named Entity] + [Specific Context]). The closest applicable primary keyword would be 'AI investment strategy' — the H1 should read something like 'AI Investment Strategy 2026: Morgan Stanley's Compute-vs-Software Forecast.' Always open the SEO guidelines and match the H1 to a keyword from Section 2 before finalizing the headline.

The Sources section lists five entries without URLs (Yahoo Finance, RBC Analysis, TradingView, Capital Group, Software Equity Group). Editorial standards require all URLs used in research to be listed at the bottom. Partial citations erode reader trust and make fact-checking impossible. Either find and include the direct URL, or restructure the inline citation to name the publication and report title precisely enough that a reader could locate it independently — e.g., 'Software Equity Group 2026 Annual SaaS Report, published March 2026.'

The H1 ('Big Tech's $700B AI Bet Strains Power Grids and Carbon Markets') is strong editorially but does not contain the primary SEO keyword 'AI data center energy costs' as required by the Marketing Director's H1 formula. On your next article, fit the target keyword into the H1 naturally — for example: 'AI Data Center Energy Costs: How Big Tech's $690B Bet Is Straining Power Grids and Carbon Markets.' You lose nothing in clarity and gain the SEO signal.

The internal link to 'fintech AI infrastructure investment' uses a placeholder URL (https://example.com/fintech-ai-infrastructure-investment) instead of the real published slug. Before submission, always replace placeholder hrefs with the actual relative slug paths defined in the SEO guidelines — in this case, /fintech-ai-infrastructure-investment. A broken internal link erodes both SEO equity and reader trust.

The article covers three distinct financial audiences — CFOs managing ESG-linked debt, utility sector investors, and AI infrastructure planners — but the conclusion addresses all three at once without differentiation. Consider flagging audience-specific takeaways with a brief label (e.g., 'For CFOs:' / 'For utility investors:') to make the piece more forwardable to a specific colleague, which is a key behavior for our C-suite readership.

TOF word count discipline: This article ran ~1,050–1,100 words against a hard ceiling of 1,000 for TOF. The 'What Specific Results Has Explainable AI Compliance Produced?' section largely restates points made in 'What Research Actually Shows' — consolidate or cut that section entirely to land inside the limit without losing substance. TOF readers need a tight, scannable brief, not a full case study.

Complete the FAQ render in the article body: The third FAQ pair ('What is the FCA Mills Review and when will it affect fintech firms?') is truncated mid-answer in the published body. The frontmatter pair is fully written — ensure the formatter pulls all three pairs through to the rendered section. Two FAQs against a declared has_faq=true is a reader-facing gap and a missed featured-snippet opportunity.

Internal link path format: Both internal links use full domain URLs (https://theparticlepost.com/...) rather than the relative slug paths specified in the SEO guidelines (e.g., /posts/fintech-ai-infrastructure-investment/). Relative paths are more resilient to domain changes and are the format the cross-link map specifies. Switch to relative markdown links for all future articles.

Em dash discipline: The FINRA/NASCUS paragraph in 'What Research Actually Shows' uses two em dashes as stylistic connectors within the same paragraph. Editorial guidelines flag repeated em dash use as an AI-generation signal. Restructure one of those clauses — make it its own sentence or use a colon — so no paragraph contains more than one em dash. This is the only stylistic issue holding the article back from a clean score.

The final FAQ answer is truncated mid-sentence ('The programme runs'). This appears to be a copy/paste artifact from the draft pipeline. Before publication, complete the fourth FAQ answer so it closes fully — readers and search crawlers both see the FAQ, and an unfinished answer undermines credibility on a piece that is otherwise tight and well-sourced.

Strong TOF execution overall: the H1 question format, 'Clear Verdict' section, answer-first structure under the first H2, and two internal links pointing down the funnel all meet spec cleanly. For your next TOF piece, consider whether the 'What Research Actually Shows' section title could be made more specific — something like 'What Visa's Pilot Data Actually Proves' would better signal topical authority and align more precisely with the long-tail SEO patterns in our H2 guidelines (Template A or B format).

INTERNAL LINKS ARE MISSING — THIS IS THE MOST URGENT FIX: As a MOF article, you must include a minimum of 2 internal markdown links: at least one pointing to a TOF article and one pointing to a BOF article. Per the SEO guidelines internal linking table, this article on AI fraud detection should link to 'fintech-ai-infrastructure-investment' using anchor text '80% of fintech firms going all-in on AI infrastructure' and to 'agentic-ai-regulatory-gap-fintech' using anchor text 'agentic AI regulatory gap in fintech'. Add these as natural inline links within the body — e.g., in the context paragraph where you discuss AI infrastructure investment or in the regulatory risk section.

H1 MUST CONTAIN A PRIMARY SEO KEYWORD FROM THE MASTER LIST: Your H1 ('What AI Fraud Detection ROI Actually Proves — and Where the Business Case Breaks Down') does not include any of the 8 primary target keywords from the SEO guidelines (e.g., 'machine learning financial services', 'AI in finance', 'artificial intelligence banking'). Rewrite the H1 to lead with a primary keyword per the SEO H1 formula — for example: 'Machine Learning Financial Services: What Visa's $40B Fraud ROI Actually Proves'. This is the single highest-leverage SEO fix you can make.

THE SOURCING AND SUBSTANCE ARE GENUINELY STRONG — PROTECT THAT CREDIBILITY WITH TIGHTER ATTRIBUTION ON THE JPMORGAN FIGURE: Your Visa and Mastercard citations are well-verified and the methodological critique (no control groups, self-reported data, period selection flattering results) is exactly the kind of analysis Particle Post readers need. However, the JPMorgan $1.5 billion figure is sourced via Modernize.io citing a LinkedIn post — not a primary source. For a claim of this magnitude, trace it to JPMorgan's own earnings call transcript, annual report, or an investor relations disclosure. A Bloomberg or Reuters report citing JPMorgan directly would also meet the standard. Modernize.io via LinkedIn does not.
